text: This word reminds me of the book *The [House]{blue} of Leaves*. Merriam Webster defines it is as *'of, relating to, or situated at a sensory threshold'*. Liminal is the vague sense that something is out there, watching us in the dark. Liminal is the careful touch of a lover after an argument, or the moment of heureka before a grand discovery: "There must be something here, I can feel it!"

Academia is a marathon. There are kilometres when the sun is smiling just right and trees provide refreshing shade, there's a spring in your step, etc. Other times, there's rain, and the wind comes down strong, and you feel weak, and there's pain in your ankles, and your side hurts, and it feels like everything is conspiring against you. But see, soon the clouds may part and you'll reach a sunny spot again.

Precise movements are required for disarming a bomb. Sweat drips from the forehead, the heartrate raises, but one needs to move slowly, carefully, lazily. Yet, precisely. Like a cat going hunting after a long sleep in a sunny spot. A long, deep breath in, and the wire is cut. Pause. For a moment, the whole world is standing still. Then a sigh. Success.

"We spared no expense." The zoo director pointed out all the fine details: the standing desks, the wide-screen monitors, the posters on the walls. The glass shades on the windows were wrestling with the sunbeams with very little hope. The visitors admired the enclosure. Some sat down, some were taking photos, and all of them were chattering merrily in their various languages. Their view was somewhat obscured by the steam of a mysterious black liquid emerging from a dozen cracked mugs inside the enclosure. The zoo director could not quite make out what happened next before he heard screams: after deleting their papers and their institutional accesses, how, one-by-one, the researchers started climbing through the fences...

"I could've sworn that the word comes from dialect, but alas! I'm a linguist, not a philosopher. I like that all that we know about the Socratic method has been written down by his student, Plato. So whose method are we learning about? If you're trying to sell ideas, maybe it's more convincing to refer to a more authoritative, if invented, source?" These words are coming from a grocery store clerk while I was trying to pay for my beer.

The assayer was a big man, and a heavy breather with an evident cold, who, every once in a while, sneezed loudly into his thick cloth tissue. The only other noises in the room emerged faintly from his trusty touchstone rubbing against shining rings, pendants, earrings, necklaces, bracelets, anklets, brooches, piercings, and more. Drops of acid shone on the stone for gold and shimmered for silver. Worthy objects received the careful hit of a hallmark; but God be merciful to the ones that didn't measure up to the Standards.
